teacher_guide.big_idea: Making a ten helps young mathematicians add more efficiently and explain their thinking clearly.
learning_goals: I can use make-ten thinking to solve an addition problem.
I can explain how I moved parts to make a ten.
I can show my answer with words, pictures, or equations.
teacher_notes: Keep student-facing language concrete and short.
Do not turn the lesson into a vocabulary quiz.
Look for whether students actually make a ten, not just whether they say the phrase.

content.subtitle: How can making a ten help us add quickly and explain our thinking?

content.scenario: There are 8 dots in one ten-frame and 5 more dots beside it. How could we make a ten first?
prompts: How many more make a ten?
What happens to the extra dots?

rows: Look for a number close to ten.
Move just enough to make a full ten.
Add the ten and the extra ones.

prompts: Why might that be easier to solve?

content.task: Solve each one by making a ten first.

prompts: One way making ten helps me is ...
When I move counters, I am ...
A problem I can solve by making ten is ...

anchor: A full ten can make adding easier.
We can move some ones to make a ten first.
worksheet.tip: Circle the number close to ten before you start.

questions.q_text: Solve 8 + 5 by making a ten. Show the ten and the extra ones.
Solve 9 + 4 by making a ten. Write the new equation.
Solve 7 + 6 by making a ten. Draw or write your thinking.
Write your own make-ten problem and solve it.

self_check: Did I make a full ten first?
Did I show the extra ones?

exit_ticket.prompt: Solve 8 + 6 by making a ten. Show your thinking with a picture, numbers, or words.

phases.label: Warm-Up
phases.teacher_move: Project ten-frame. Ask: what do you notice? Cold-call 2–3 students. No evaluation yet.
phases.student_move: Look, notice, share one observation with a partner.

phases.label: Model
phases.teacher_move: Think aloud: move counters to make a ten, then count on. Show two examples. Name the strategy.
phases.student_move: Watch and track the moves — raise a hand when they see the ten being made.

phases.label: Guided Practice
phases.teacher_move: Release to partners. Circulate. Listen for students saying 'I moved __ to make a ten.' Redirect if adding without making ten.
phases.student_move: Solve 3 problems with a partner using ten-frames. One person moves, one explains.
phases.checkpoint: By min 35: most pairs have solved at least 2 problems and can say what they moved.

phases.label: Independent Work
phases.teacher_move: Release to worksheet. Pull a small group if any students struggled in guided practice.
phases.student_move: Complete worksheet independently. Draw pictures or write equations to show thinking.

phases.label: Exit Ticket & Share
phases.teacher_move: Collect exit tickets at the door. Choose 1–2 to share under document camera (ask permission first).
phases.student_move: Complete exit ticket. Optional: share strategy with class.

pacing_guide.buffer_note: If guided practice runs long, shorten independent work to 10 min and skip the whole-class share at the end.

sub_plan.title: Making Ten to Add — Sub Plan
sub_plan.class_context: Second-grade math. Students have been learning the make-ten strategy for addition. They know how to use ten-frames and can explain their moves with a partner. This is a standard lesson day — nothing new is being introduced.
sub_plan.materials_location: Blue folder on my desk labeled 'Sub Plans'. Slide deck is open on the teacher laptop (password on sticky note inside folder). Worksheets and exit tickets are pre-printed in the folder.
sub_plan.classroom_notes: Students sit in table groups of 3–4. Raise your voice slightly for transitions — they respond well to a count-down from 5. Two students (front-left table) have IEPs and may need extra time on the worksheet; just check in with them at the independent work step.

steps.label: Warm-Up
steps.what_to_do: Project Slide 1 (the ten-frame). Ask students to look quietly for 10 seconds, then share what they notice with a partner.
steps.what_to_say: Look at this ten-frame. You have 10 seconds to notice something — anything at all. Then turn and tell your partner what you see.
steps.what_students_do: Look at the ten-frame, think quietly, then share one observation with a partner.

steps.label: Model
steps.what_to_do: Use Slide 2. Read the think-aloud notes on the slide. Move counters (or draw) to show how to make a ten before adding. Do two examples.
steps.what_to_say: Watch me. I have 8 and 5. I need to make a ten first. I'll move 2 from the 5 over to the 8 — now I have 10 and 3 left. 10 plus 3 is 13. Did you see what I did? I made a ten first.
steps.what_students_do: Watch the model. Students may raise a hand when they see the ten being made.

steps.label: Guided Practice
steps.what_to_do: Hand out the worksheet. Students work problems 1–3 with a partner. Circulate and listen. If a pair finishes early, ask them to explain their thinking out loud.
steps.what_to_say: Work with your partner on problems 1 through 3. One person moves, one person explains. Switch for the next problem.
steps.what_students_do: Solve 3 problems with a partner, taking turns moving and explaining.

steps.label: Independent Work
steps.what_to_do: Students complete the rest of the worksheet on their own. Circulate quietly. Do not give answers — ask 'what could you make a ten with?'
steps.what_to_say: Now finish the rest of the worksheet on your own. If you get stuck, look at what you did with your partner and try the same moves.

steps.label: Exit Ticket
steps.what_to_do: Distribute exit tickets. Give students 5 minutes to complete. Collect at the door as they leave. Leave completed exit tickets in the tray on my desk.
steps.what_to_say: We have about 8 minutes left. Put your worksheet face-down and pick up an exit ticket. Complete it on your own — this is just for you to show what you know.
steps.what_students_do: Complete the exit ticket independently, then hand it in on the way out.

sub_plan.if_stuck: If students are confused during guided practice, slow down and do one more model together as a class before releasing them again. If the slide deck won't open, write the problems on the board: 8+5, 6+7, 9+4. The worksheet and exit ticket do not require slides.
